title: Developing and Assessing the Validity of a Metric to Quantify the Competitiveness of Surgical Residency Programs
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"jgty8","text":"As surgical residencies become increasingly competitive, efforts are needed to improve the definition and measurement of competitiveness. Historically, most applicants utilize match rate to determine how competitive a residency is. However, there is limited data validating the use of match rate as a metric for competitiveness, and such use can lead to applicant self-selection. Metrics to measure competitiveness have been created by other studies; however, such metrics are specific to fellowships or are not straightforward enough for general use. An applicant's competitiveness is often determined based on metrics, such as the United States Licensing Exam (USMLE) Step 1 and Step 2, letters of recommendation, clerkship grades, research, and volunteering. In addition to match rate, applicants often use metrics of prior matched applicants to determine how competitive a specialty is. While metrics have continuously increased over time, it is unknown how applicant metrics correlate with the standard methods of assessing competitiveness, such as match rate. The aim of this study was to develop and validate a simple metric to assess the competitiveness of general and subspecialty surgical residency programs.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"860pg","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"nrylz","text":"Surgical residency has become increasingly competitive as the demand for physicians rises.1,2 As surgical residencies continue to grow, there are ongoing efforts to improve the definition of competitiveness. Most applicants utilize match rate to determine how competitive a residency is.3,4 However, there is limited data validating the use of match rate or available positions as a metric for competitiveness.3 Additionally, Lefebvre et al found that utilizing match rate to determine competitiveness can result in applicant self-selection.3 Cobb et al developed a metric using the number of positions and applications to assess competitiveness; however, this metric does not account for match rate and may not be feasible for applicants.1 Similarly, Vaysberg et al created a competitiveness index using the percentage of filled programs divided by the match rate for fellowships.2 The percentage of filled programs is often used as a way to determine fellowship competitiveness; however, this is not an optimal assessment for residency programs as the average percent filled is 99.9% .2,5","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":90,"length":3},{"style":"sup","offset":287,"length":3},{"style":"sup","offset":410,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":541,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":739,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":881,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":1087,"length":3}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"xpnxu","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"lqrlg","text":"Competitive applicants have been historically determined based on the United States Licensing Exam (USMLE) Step 1 and Step 2, letters of recommendation, clerkship grades, and extracurricular activities, such as research or volunteering.6,7With the change to Step 1 becoming pass/fail, the importance placed on alternative applicant metrics, such as Step 2, will likely occur.8 A study by Pontell et al aimed to assess the perspective of surgical program directors (PDs) regarding a pass/fail Step 1 score and found that nearly 90% of PDs stated Step 2 would increase in importance for determining applicant competitiveness.9 Many argued of unintended consequences, including fewer objective measures to distinguish applicants. Along with Step 2, letters of recommendation and research will likely be more heavily weighted.8,9 In addition to match rate, applicants often use metrics of prior matched applicants to determine how competitive a specialty is.3However, despite continuously increasing over time, it is unknown how applicant metrics correlate with the standard methods of assessing competitiveness, such as positions available or match rate.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":236,"length":3},{"style":"sup","offset":375,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":623,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":822,"length":3},{"style":"sup","offset":954,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"v2a2x","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"tx2xx","text":"The aim of this study was to develop and validate a simple metric to assess the competitiveness of general and subspecialty surgical residency programs.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"jcxmr","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Variables were selected based on an extensive literature review of relevant metrics that correlate with competitiveness. 1–4,7–12

The variables chosen included: - Match rate - Matched applicants - Total applicants - Residency positions are available each year - Number of programs ranked per applicant - United States Medical Licensing Exam (USMLE) Step 1 score - USMLE Step 2 score - Mean applicant research experiences - Mean applicant research output
Variables were collected for general surgery residency programs over the course of 20 years. Descriptive data was conducted prior to equation formation to understand trends.
Variables that significantly changed over the study period were used in equation development.
The number of programs ranked per applicant were chosen based on the theory that applicants increasing the number of ranked programs is a consequence of increasing competitiveness.
Positions were chosen in relationship with match rate due to the fact a higher applicant to position ratio results in higher competitiveness.

Match rate was used in each equation to contribute matched applicants and total applicants. While we theorized match rate is not a reliable competitiveness metric alone, the ratio of matched applicants to total applicants is necessary to include. We sought to determine the relationship of match rate with other variables for a consistent competitiveness metric.

Due to variability over time, the equations required normalization. Additionally, normalization to a value of 1 allows the user to interpret the data more easily. - However, it is important to note that the intended application is used without normalization with a set numerical definition.

To create a normalized competitiveness index (NCI): - Each CI (Equations 1-4) was averaged over time. - The yearly CI was divided by the averaged CI. - Values over one indicate higher competitiveness.
The gold standard for defining competitiveness is currently match rate.
The relationship between NCI equations (Equations 1-4) and match rate were analyzed.
The change in applicants for each year were calculated (Δ Applicants).
The change in positions for each year were calculated (Δ Positions).

The Δ Applicants was subtracted by the Δ Positions to estimate the annual change in position deficit. - The median position deficit was 15 (IQR -53 to 103) - Years that had a change in position deficit greater than 103 (>75 th percentile) or less than -53 (<25 th percentile) were marked to examine for potential change in competitiveness. - For example, years with 100 new applicants without a corresponding change in positions or 100 fewer applicants (i.e., 100 available spots).

The marked years were qualitatively assessed for each NCI. Theoretically, a change greater than +103 would result in increased competitiveness seen by a higher NCI, and a change less than -53 would lead to a lower NCI for that year.

While this method was not used in final decision-making, it allowed for a general overview of the equations with trends in time. - Figure 2 demonstrates examples of marked years with increased or decreased Δ Applicants - Δ Positions.
The normalized general surgery match rate (Figure 1A) and normalized applicants per position were plotted over 20 years (Figure 1B) and a linear regression was performed.

Each NCI equation was plotted over 20 years (Figure 2) and a linear regression was performed. - The slopes of the change in match rate were compared to the slope of the NCI using each equation. - The slopes of the NCI equations were all significantly different than the match rate. - Normalized match rate slope = 0.002 - Equation 1 slope = 0.005, p<0.001 - Equation 2 slope = 0.02, p<0.001 - Equation 3 slope = 0.05, p<0.001 - Equation 4 slope = 0.03, p<0.001 - The change in NCI each year was greater than the match rate.
The normalized general surgery match rate and each NCI equation were analyzed using a Pearson correlation over time.
There were no significant correlations to match rate when each equation was individually assessed. - All Pearson r values were between -0.05-0.50 (Figure 3).

USMLE Step 1, Step 2, research experiences, research output, work experiences, and volunteer experiences were analyzed across a 20-year period for general surgery and a 10-year period for sub-specialty surgery.
USMLE Step 2 and research output were variables that had the most consistent growth over time.
Each general surgery NCI equation (Equations 1-4) was correlated with Step 2 (Figure 4).

All four equations positively correlated with Step 2; however, Equation 3 had the strongest correlation (Pearson r=0.89, p<0.001) (Figure 4C). As a reference, the Pearson r for the normalized match rate versus Step 2 was 0.34 (p=0.41).
Research output was defined as the number of publications, presentations, and abstracts.

Each general surgery NCI equation (Equations 1-4) were correlated with research output (Figure 5). All four equations were positively correlated with research output. Equation 3 had the strongest correlation (Pearson r=0.97, p<0.001). (Figure 5C). As a reference, the Pearson r for the normalized match rate versus research output was 0.56 (p=0.12).

Sub-specialty surgical residencies were selected based on their relationship to general surgery. - Currently, there are three specialties that can be completed as either an integrated residency program or fellowship following general surgery residency: Plastic Surgery, Thoracic Surgery, and Vascular Surgery. - Orthopedic Surgery, Neurosurgery, Otolaryngology, and Obstetrics and Gynecology were not selected based on fellowship options following general surgery residency.
All four equations were analyzed in relationship to match rate, applicants per position, and applicant metrics for each specialty.
Overall, match rate remained constant over time at 64% ± 7 (R 2 =0.10, p=0.37).

All equations were significantly different across time compared to match rate. - Normalized match rate slope = 0.01 - Equation 1 slope = 0.02, p<0.001 - Equation 2 slope = 0.5, p<0.001 - Equation 3 slope = 0.08, p<0.001 - Equation 4 slope = 0.07, p<0.00

Match rate was not correlated with any of the equations (Pearson r range -0.1 to 0.50) Step 2 scores were not correlated with any of the equations (range -0.01 to -0.31) Research output was correlated with equation 1 (r=0.74), equation 2 (r=0.72), and equation 3 (0.87). Data are displayed in Figure 6.
Results were similar with no significant change in match rate over time (51% ± 6, R 2 =0.001, p=0.99).

Match rates were not correlated with any of the equations (r=-0.6 to 0.10). Step 2 scores were not correlated with any of the equations (range -0.05 to 0.27) Research output was correlated with equation 1 (r=0.69), equation 2 (r=0.84), and equation 3 (0.94). Data are displayed in Figure 7.
Results were similar with no significant change in match rate over time (31% ± 4, R 2 =0.08, p=0.41).
Match rates were not correlated with any of the equations (r=-0.13 to 0.20). The sample size for applicant metrics was not sufficient for analysis as data were only available from 2019-2021.

1. Higher values of the CI are expected to be associated with greater competitiveness; however, future research should focus on defining a scale for easy interpretation. 2. The CI is expected to provide a more realistic view of the current competitiveness of residency programs and is designed to be applicable to all residency programs. - If the intention is to look over time, using an NCI should be considered.

Figure 1. General surgery match data from 2003-2022: (A) the normalized match rate (i.e., yearly match rate divided by averaged match rate) demonstrating little change over time (R 2 =0.02, p=57) and (B) normalized applicants per position demonstrated little change over time (R 2 =0.01, p=76). Figure 2. The four competitive indices were normalized and plotted across time with linear regressions for general surgery residency: (A) Equation one (number of programs ranked per applicant divided by match rate) over time, (B) Equation two (number of programs ranked per applicant multiplied by positions divided by match rate) over time, (C) Equation three (positions over match rate) over time, and (D) Equation four (positions multiplied by matched applicants divided by total applicants) over time. The arrows on the x-axis represent years there were significant changes in the ratio of new applicants to new positions. The R 2 &nbsp;and p-value of all correlations are displayed. Figure 3. The normalized competitive index (NCI) of all four equations were correlated with match rate for general surgery residency: (A) Equation one (number of programs ranked per applicant divided by match rate) versus match rate, (B) Equation two (number of programs ranked per applicant multiplied by positions divided by match rate) versus match rate, (C) Equation three (positions over match rate) versus match rate, and (D) Equation four (positions multiplied by matched applicants divided by total applicants) versus match rate. The Pearson r, R 2 , and p-value of all correlations are displayed. Figure 4. The normalized competitive index (NCI) of all four equations were correlated with match rate for general surgery residency: (A) Equation one (number of programs ranked per applicant divided by match rate) versus Step 2, (B) Equation two (number of programs ranked per applicant multiplied by positions divided by match rate) versus Step 2, (C) Equation three (positions over match rate) versus Step 2, and (D) Equation four (positions multiplied by matched applicants divided by total applicants) versus Step 2. The Pearson r, R 2 , and p-value of all correlations are displayed. Figure 5. The normalized competitive index (NCI) of all four equations were correlated with match rate for general surgery residency: (A) Equation one (number of programs ranked per applicant divided by match rate) versus research output, (B) Equation two (number of programs ranked per applicant multiplied by positions divided by match rate) versus research output, (C) Equation three (positions over match rate) versus research output, and (D) Equation four (positions multiplied by matched applicants divided by total applicants) versus research output. The Pearson r, R 2 , and p-value of all correlations are displayed. Figure 6. Ten years of plastic surgery residency match data were used to validate the four competitive indices (CI). These graphs display equation 3, which was chosen as the most accurate CI. (A) Equation 3 (positions divided by match rate) over time, (B) equation 3 NCI correlated over time with match rate, (C) the NCI correlated over time with Step 2, and the NCI correlated over time with research output. The Pearson r, R 2 , and p-value of all correlations are displayed. Figure 7. Ten years of vascular surgery residency match data were used to validate the four competitive indices (CI). These graphs display equation 3, which was chosen as the most accurate CI. (A) Equation 3 (positions divided by match rate) over time, (B) equation 3 NCI correlated over time with match rate, (C) the NCI correlated over time with Step 2, and the NCI correlated over time with research output. The Pearson r, R 2 , and p-value of all correlations are displayed.
